cataloging_subject.label: Merlin
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Herry Lovelich's single Middle English verse romance Merlin as preserved in its unique, end-defective witness: 27,852 extant lines. CME00134 represents only lines 1-15,556 from Ernst A. Kock's Parts I-II, not the complete surviving work. The medieval work, its French source tradition, manuscript witness, Kock's three publication installments, University of Michigan encoding, repository XML, and digital facsimiles remain distinct descriptive and lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: Merlin
catalog_summary.author: Herry Lovelich
catalog_summary.date: Probably composed or translated in the 1420s; traditionally dated c.1450; witness dated broadly to the fifteenth century by James and c.1450-75 by Parker
catalog_summary.region: Probably London, England; the unique witness has an Essex dialect profile

identifiers.value: Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library MS 80

titles.value: Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance

scope.description: Modern title of Kock's three-part edition, inherited by CME's encoded title page

titles.value: Now gyneth the devel to wraththen him sore

scope.description: First verse line as encoded; the final n of wraththen is an italic editorial expansion in CME/Kock

titles.value: Now ginneth the devil to wrathen him sore
notes: No explicit is supplied because the unique witness loses an unknown number of leaves at the end. CME00134's last line is only the endpoint of the encoded selection.

agents.name: Herry Lovelich
notes: London skinner and Middle English poet. Herry is the form used by Kock and Ackerman; Henry is the modern authority form used by DMR and DIMEV.

agents.name: Ernst A. Kock
notes: Editor of the three textual installments published by the Early English Text Society in 1904, 1913, and 1932.

scope.description: Authorship of the extant 27,852-line Middle English verse romance
Translation of the French prose Vulgate Merlin into Middle English

notes: No surviving physical French exemplar is identified.

scope.description: Versification and adaptation of French prose narrative into rhyming Middle English couplets
Modern three-part source edition of all 27,852 surviving lines; CME00134 derives only from Parts I-II

notes: Kock's chapter divisions, marginal summaries, expansions, punctuation, and notes belong to the editorial layer, not to Lovelich's authorial responsibility.
DMR gives the 1420s. Ackerman reports an argument for about 1425 tied to the death of Lovelich's patron Henry Barton in 1434.
Retained as a competing traditional date rather than silently merged with DMR's 1420s composition date or the later witness date.

scope.description: James's broad date for Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library MS 80, not a date of composition
Date of Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library MS 80, not necessarily date of composition

notes: Retained separately from James's broader fifteenth-century manuscript date and from both competing work-composition dates.

scope.description: Furnivall's 146-line Mordred excerpt from manuscript f.135, column 1

notes: DIMEV's bibliography key Furnivall1861 reflects the larger series context, but the inspected volume II title page containing this appendix is dated 1863.

date_statements.display: first publication of the opening 1,638 lines, 1890
complete extant text published in installments, 1904-1932
notes: Part I appeared in 1904, Part II in 1913, and Part III in 1932. Completion of the surviving text does not restore the manuscript's lost ending or the edition's promised critical apparatus.
DMR assigns London as the place of composition. Parker links Lovelich to the London Skinners' Company; this contextual association is not itself proof of where every line was composed.

scope.description: LALME/eLALME dialect localization of the language of the unique witness

notes: DIMEV associates manuscript CamCCC80 with LALME/eLALME linguistic profile 6360 and Essex. This is a witness-dialect statement, not a competing claim that Lovelich composed the work in Essex.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: French is the language of the source work, not an additional primary language of Lovelich's romance. Modern English, German, and French editorial matter in source editions is likewise not cataloged as language of the work.

form_statements.description: 27,852 extant Middle English lines in rhyming couplets; DMR summarizes the rhyme as aa4bb4 and notes metrical variation.

subject_statements.label: Merlin as prophet, counselor, and enchanter
King Arthur's conception, accession, and reign
Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
The Round Table and Arthurian fellowship
Religious figures and spaces, sacrament, pilgrimage, and penance
Travel through forests, cities, courts, and secular spaces

summaries.value: Merlin is Herry Lovelich's long Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the French prose Vulgate Merlin. Its 27,852 surviving lines narrate Merlin's demonic conception and Christian rescue, his prophetic and political service, Uther's reign and Arthur's conception, Arthur's accession and wars, the establishment of the Round Table, the betrothal to Guinevere, and later Arthurian campaigns and relationships. The unique manuscript ends imperfectly about halfway through the extant French source tradition, so neither the original English ending nor the extent Lovelich intended can be recovered from the sole witness.

The narrative covers Merlin's origins and education; Vortiger and the tower; Uther Pendragon; Arthur's conception, fosterage, accession, and battles; baronial and foreign conflict; the Round Table; Arthur's marriage to Guinevere or Gonnore; Gawain and other princes; Merlin's dealings with Nimiane; and the wars of Ban of Benoic and allied rulers. Themes indexed by DMR include disguise, exile, incest, marriage, military combat, siege, treachery, supernatural intervention, penance, pilgrimage, sacrament, merchants, and travel.

CME00134 encodes only Kock's lines 1-15,556, corresponding to all of Parts I-II and manuscript f.88v through part of f.146v. It stops mid-work and mid-sentence; 12,296 further extant lines appear in Kock Part III. In the XML, the first top-level DIV1 is title-page paratext and the second top-level DIV1 is the poem; fourteen DIV2 children within the poem wrapper are Kock's editorial chapters, not medieval authorial sections.

The preferred title, authorship, date, place, form, genre, and subjects describe Lovelich's work. Manuscript date and dialect, editorial chaptering and expansion, scan coverage, digital encoding structure, and provider rights are attached only to their appropriate witness, edition, or representation layers through lineage and scoped statements.

extent.description: One verse work of 27,852 surviving lines in rhyming couplets. Its unique witness is end-defective, so the lost English ending and intended total extent are unknown. CME00134 contains 15,556 nonempty L elements (lines 1-15,556), leaving 12,296 extant lines outside the encoding. Its 268 LG elements and fourteen DIV2 chapters are encoding/editorial metrics, not a complete stanza count or evidence of medieval sectioning; seven explicit L/@N values diverge from ordinal XML position.

content_parts.label: Extant Merlin
content_parts.summary: The entire surviving Middle English romance, preserved uniquely and imperfectly in CCCC MS 80 and printed by Kock across three installments.

locators.value: Parker broad component ff.88v-200v; surviving Merlin in current physical order ff.88v-196v + ff.199r-200v, with displaced Holy Grail leaves at current ff.197-198

notes: DIMEV gives ff.88v-196v, while Parker catalogs ff.88v-200v. James's collation demonstrates that current ff.197-198 belong to the preceding Holy Grail and that current ff.199-200 are surviving Merlin leaves.

content_parts.label: CME-encoded opening: lines 1-15,556
content_parts.summary: The opening 55.9 percent of the extant line count, encoded from Kock Parts I-II and ending mid-narrative within manuscript f.146v.

locators.value: (//DIV1)[2] (poem wrapper), with editorial chapters at (//DIV1)[2]/DIV2[1-14]; (//DIV1)[1] is title-page paratext and is excluded from verse metrics
f.88v through part of f.146v; Parker canvases 184-300

notes: The 268 LG elements are XML groupings, not a normalized couplet or stanza count. The fourteen chapters and their headings are Kock's modern navigation layer. The XML retains the Part I title page but silently crosses to Part II after four unnumbered PB elements.

content_parts.label: Surviving continuation absent from CME00134: lines 15,557-27,852
content_parts.summary: The remaining 12,296 extant lines printed in Kock Part III, continuing immediately after the CME endpoint and ending where the unique manuscript is defective.

locators.value: remainder of f.146v through current f.196v, then current ff.199r-200v; exact line-to-folio crosswalk across the physical interruption remains to be verified

notes: This is an analytical representation segment, not an authorial medieval section. Kock Part III prints the complete extant continuation, not the lost medieval ending.

related_works.title: French Vulgate Merlin
notes: Lovelich translates and versifies approximately the first half of the French prose work as it survives in English. No physical French exemplar is known, and it is unknown whether he translated beyond the lost English ending.

evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD/REVDESCR; IDG; FRONT/(//DIV1)[1]; BODY/(//DIV1)[2]; (//DIV1)[2]/DIV2[1-14]; L, LG, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, HI, and SUP
evidence.summary: Supplies the encoded identifier, Kock Part I title page, author and editor forms, 15,556-line verse selection, fourteen editorial chapters, printed-page and manuscript-column milestones, marginal and footnote apparatus, expansion typography, and review history. It distinguishes the first title-page DIV1 wrapper from the second poem DIV1 wrapper.

evidence.citation: Structural, line-number, page-boundary, and source comparison of CME00134.xml
evidence.locator: 2 top-level DIV1 wrappers; 14 poem DIV2; 15,556 body L; 268 LG; 234 MILESTONE; 4,222 NOTE; 421 PB; 7 P
evidence.summary: Confirms that (//DIV1)[1] is title-page paratext and (//DIV1)[2] is the poem, with chapters at (//DIV1)[2]/DIV2[1-14]. Counts 15,556 nonempty lines, maps Parts I-II and manuscript f.88v through f.146v, identifies the silent Part II boundary, and finds seven L/@N values differing from ordinal XML position.

evidence.citation: Ernst A. Kock, ed., Merlin, Parts I-II, EETS Extra Series 93 and 112 (1904, 1913)
evidence.summary: The complete inspected source scan confirms lines 1-15,556, printed pp.1-416, exact Part I-II boundaries, the 1904 and 1913 publication installments, Kock's editorial formatting, and the fact that CME stops mid-work.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Merlin
evidence.summary: Supports the 1420s and London composition claims, rhyming-couplet form, IMEV 2312, unique witness, lost ending and unknown original extent, French Vulgate Merlin source, detailed plot summary, and subject keywords.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse version 1.0, record 3734
evidence.summary: Supports title, Henry Lovelich authority form, 27,852-line couplet extent, IMEV/NIMEV 2312, Arthurian literature and romance classifications, unique witness statement, Essex witness-dialect profile, three-part Kock edition, and earlier Kölbing and Furnivall excerpts.

evidence.citation: Parker Library on the Web, Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, MS 080
evidence.summary: Supports the unique manuscript identity, Parker's c.1450-75 witness date, broad ff.88v-200v component, end-defective state, and Lovelich's London/Skinners' Company association; it does not establish London as the manuscript's copying place. It also supports translation and versification from French prose.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, catalogue record for CCCC MS 80
evidence.summary: Supports manuscript date and physical description and resolves the current-foliation interruption: ff.197-198 are displaced Holy Grail leaves, while ff.199-200 are the two surviving terminal Merlin leaves.

evidence.citation: Parker Library complete 414-canvas IIIF facsimile of CCCC MS 80
evidence.summary: Supports complete-object coverage and exact mapping of the CME selection from canvas 184, f.88v, through canvas 300, f.146v; the full manuscript bundle is cached privately rather than a crop of the target work.

Supports the preferred title, traditional c.1450 date, English romance classification, 27,852-line extent, French prose Roman de Merlin adaptation, witness and edition bibliography, and Wikidata identifier.

evidence.citation: Robert W. Ackerman, 'Herry Lovelich's Merlin,' PMLA 67.4 (1952): 473-484
evidence.summary: Supports Herry Lovelich attribution, competing c.1450 and suggested c.1425 dating, the manuscript description, the three Kock installments, and the distinction between completion of the printed text and the uncompleted promised critical apparatus.

evidence.citation: Eugen Kölbing's 1890 edition of Lovelich's Merlin, lines 1-1,638
evidence.summary: Supports the 1890 partial publication of the opening 1,638 lines. The independent transcription is a supporting predecessor, not an asserted ancestor of Kock's edition.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall's 1863 146-line Mordred excerpt from Lovelich's Merlin
evidence.summary: Supports the first verified excerpt publication: 146 lines from manuscript f.135 column 1, printed with parallel French in the 1863 volume. It is not modeled as an ancestor of Kock's edition.

open_questions.question: Which physical French manuscript or textual subfamily supplied Lovelich's Vulgate Merlin exemplar?
next_steps: Consult textual studies comparing Lovelich's readings with Vulgate Merlin manuscript families.
Record a physical French witness only when an edition or study establishes the relationship rather than inferring it from the work-level source.

open_questions.question: Did Lovelich translate beyond the point where the unique manuscript now ends, and what was the original English ending?
next_steps: Search for lost-fragment reports, later copies, citations, and textual references to a fuller Lovelich Merlin.
Keep the 27,852-line extent labeled extant rather than complete or authorially final.

open_questions.question: How securely can the 1420s and London composition claims be distinguished from the traditional c.1450 date and the manuscript's Essex dialect profile?
next_steps: Review the full arguments behind DMR's dating and localization.
Separate patronage biography, author residence, composition location, copying date, and dialect localization in any refinement.

open_questions.question: What is the exact Kock Part III line-to-folio and facsimile-canvas crosswalk after f.146v, especially across the displaced current ff.197-198?
next_steps: Inspect a complete lawful copy of Kock Part III against Parker canvases 301-408.
Map each folio-column milestone and verify the transition from current f.196v to f.199r without treating displaced Holy Grail leaves as Merlin.

notes: The work-level record describes all 27,852 surviving lines. CME00134 is an incomplete digital representation of that work, even though it completely encodes the textual portions of Kock Parts I-II.

The manuscript is extant but end-defective. Kock Part III completes the edition of what survives; it neither reconstructs the lost English ending nor proves that Lovelich stopped at the surviving endpoint.

The XML has two top-level DIV1 wrappers: (//DIV1)[1] title-page paratext and (//DIV1)[2] the poem. Verse and chapter metrics must target the second wrapper; its fourteen DIV2 units are editorial chapters.
The final encoded line, 'j schal ȝow tellen, with-owten ony lette.', is not an explicit. It is followed by 12,296 surviving lines outside CME00134.

Parker's complete manuscript facsimile is locally cached in a Git-ignored directory under CC BY-NC 4.0 terms. Commercial reuse of Parker images requires attention to provider licensing and, where appropriate, direct permission; those rights attach to the images, not to the medieval text's descriptive metadata.
The exact French source work is established at the textual-tradition level, but no source-manuscript witness is invented.
